system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 8: Chord Reference Charts

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 8 — Chord Reference Charts

_Pages 17-54._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Chord synonyms defined

> There are some chords with different names but the same notes, and they may be used in place of
> each other.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines synonyms as substitutable enharmonic chord-name equivalents.

## Synonyms support substitution

> The main benefit of knowing chord synonyms is that you can use them to come up with substitute
> chords you wouldn’t have come up with otherwise.

**Significance (p. 17).** Establishes the practical purpose of learning synonyms — generating substitutions.

## add 9+ ≡ 7+ rule

> No add 9+ chords are listed; they are the same as 7+ chords whose roots are 2 whole steps higher.

**Significance (p. 17).** Specific synonym rule eliminating an entire category from the reference.

## Dominant family is largest

> There are more dominant chords, by far, than major or minor types.

**Significance (p. 17).** Justifies the disproportionate page allocation in the reference charts.

## 13b9 omission rule

> Sometimes 13b9 chords are played with no b7 or no 3rd - not both.

**Significance (p. 17).** Specific tone-omission rule preserving identity of altered dominant.

## 9 suspended derivation

> When the 3rd is left out, this chord is also called a 9 suspended.

**Significance (p. 17).** Defines 9-sus as 9-chord-without-3rd.

## Augmented symmetry

> Augmented chords repeat every 4 frets.

**Significance (p. 52).** States the symmetry property that gives augmented chords 3 names.

## +11 vs 9b5

> This chord is very similar to the 95 chord. The only difference is that the Sth and the +11th (Sth)
> are in the +11 chord while just the +1 1th (Sth) is in the 95.

**Significance (p. 52).** Distinguishes augmented-11th from flat-5 dominant by tone content.

## 9b5 preferred over 9#5b5

> 9b5’s have a less muddy sound and are more easily used

**Significance (p. 52).** Practical preference for 9b5 over the curious 9#5b5 chord.

## 9#5b5 single inversion

> There seems to be only one inversion of this amazing chord

**Significance (p. 52).** Notes the exceptional case of a chord with only one playable inversion.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
